attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-day history of low back pain after gardening over the weekend. She describes the pain as a dull ache that worsens with movement. She has no radiation of pain to her legs, no bowel or bladder symptoms, and no numbness in her lower limbs. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. She works as an office administrator and has been unable to sit comfortably at her desk.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. She has paraspinal muscle tenderness and reduced range of movement of her lumbar spine due to pain. Straight leg raise is negative bilaterally. Neurological examination of her lower limbs is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first-line non-pharmacological management for this patient?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with acute, non-specific low back pain without any red flags suggesting serious pathology. The absence of radicular symptoms, normal neurological examination, and negative straight leg raise test indicate that this is likely a simple musculoskeletal strain. In such cases, non-pharmacological approaches are recommended as first-line management.

Heat therapy is an appropriate first-line non-pharmacological management for acute low back pain. Heat increases blood flow to the affected area, reduces pain, and helps muscles to relax, which can improve mobility. Heat can be applied using wheat bags, heat wraps, heat pads, or hot water bottles for short periods (e.g., 30 minutes) as needed. Evidence suggests that heat therapy can provide short-term pain relief and improve function in acute low back pain.

Bed rest for 48 hours is not recommended for acute low back pain. Current evidence strongly supports remaining active rather than resting in bed. Prolonged bed rest can lead to muscle deconditioning, which may worsen pain and delay recovery. Patients should be encouraged to continue normal activities as tolerated.

Ice baths are too extreme for treating low back pain and may cause significant discomfort. While localised cold therapy might be helpful in the first 24-48 hours if there is significant inflammation, full-body ice baths are not appropriate and could potentially worsen muscle spasm in the affected area.

Mindfulness meditation may be a useful adjunct for pain management in chronic conditions but is not typically recommended as a first-line approach for acute mechanical low back pain. While it may help with pain perception and coping, it does not address the underlying musculoskeletal issue as directly as heat therapy.

In the very acute stage (first few days), gentle movement within pain limits is encouraged, but a structured exercise programme may be too intense and could exacerbate symptoms. Once the initial severe pain has subsided, a graduated exercise programme including gentle stretching, core strengthening, and mobility exercises becomes an important component of recovery and prevention of recurrence.
learningPoints: Heat therapy is effective for reducing pain and improving function in acute low back pain.
Heat therapy for low back pain works by increasing blood flow to the affected area and promoting muscle relaxation.
Various heat therapy options for low back pain include wheat bags, heat wraps, heat pads, and hot water bottles, each with specific safety considerations.
references: Michaleff, Z.  RACGP Handbook of Non-Drug Interventions: Heat therapy for low back pain. 14 September 2021. Accessed at: https://www.racgp.org.au/clinical-resources/clinical-guidelines/handi/a-z/h/heat-therapy-for-low-back-pain

Parr A, Askin G. Non-radicular low back pain: Assessment and evidence-based treatment. Aust J Gen Pract. 2020 Nov;49(11):724-727. doi: 10.31128/AJGP-06-20-5476. PMID: 33123711. Accessed at: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2020/november/non-radicular-low-back-pain
